title: ZPA Application Segment Health
spl:
index=zt sourcetype="zscaler:zpa" earliest=-24h
| where match(lower(status),"(?i)fail|error|down") OR latency_ms>2000
| stats count by app_segment, connector_group, error_code
| sort 30 -count
description: Tracks connector health, app segment reachability, and error rates for ZPA-published apps.
value: ZPA administrators monitor application segment health by tracking connector availability, user access latency, and SLA compliance, ensuring each ZTNA-published application has sufficient connector capacity to serve users without degradation.
implementation: Normalize `app_segment` and latency fields from your ZPA TA. Alert on connector group with >5% error rate vs prior week.
visualization: Table (unhealthy segments), Line chart (error rate), Single value (segments in alert).
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication
  by Authentication.user, Authentication.action, Authentication.src span=1h
| sort - count
knownFalsePositives: Anomalies from new SaaS app rollouts, browser updates, or vendor URL category changes.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Zscaler Private Access (ZPA) logs via LSS to Splunk. Data in `index=zscaler` with `sourcetype=zscaler:zpa:user_activity` and `sourcetype=zscaler:zpa:connector`. Key fields (user activity): `Application`, `ApplicationSegment`, `Action`, `User`, `ConnectorGroup`, `ServerIP`, `DoubleEncryption`, `latency_ms`. Key fields (connector): `ConnectorName`, `ConnectorStatus`, `ConnectorGroupName`.
• ZPA replaces VPN with per-application access tunnels. Each application segment has App Connectors that broker connections. If connectors go offline, the application becomes unreachable even though the user passes all policy checks.
• Create a `zpa_app_segments.csv` lookup: `segment_name,app_owner,sla_target_ms,min_connectors_required`.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Configure ZPA LSS: ZPA Admin > App Connectors > Log Streaming Service > add Splunk receiver.

Verify:
```spl
index=zscaler sourcetype="zscaler:zpa:connector" earliest=-1h
| stats count by ConnectorName, ConnectorStatus
```

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

Primary search — Application segment health:
```spl
index=zscaler sourcetype="zscaler:zpa:connector" earliest=-24h
| dedup ConnectorName sortby -_time
| stats count(eval(ConnectorStatus="Active")) as active count(eval(ConnectorStatus!="Active")) as inactive by ConnectorGroupName
| lookup zpa_app_segments.csv segment_name as ConnectorGroupName OUTPUT min_connectors_required app_owner
| eval health=if(active >= tonumber(coalesce(min_connectors_required, 1)), "Healthy", "DEGRADED")
| sort health
```

Understanding this SPL: Shows whether each application segment has enough active connectors. A segment with fewer than the minimum required connectors is degraded — users may experience slow access or complete failure if remaining connectors are overloaded. Connectors go offline due to: host resource exhaustion, network issues, or ZPA agent updates.

Application access latency:
```spl
index=zscaler sourcetype="zscaler:zpa:user_activity" earliest=-24h
| stats avg(latency_ms) as avg_latency perc95(latency_ms) as p95_latency count as accesses by ApplicationSegment
| lookup zpa_app_segments.csv segment_name as ApplicationSegment OUTPUT sla_target_ms
| eval sla_met=if(p95_latency <= tonumber(coalesce(sla_target_ms, 500)), "MET", "MISSED")
| sort - p95_latency
```

Step 3 — Validate
(a) In ZPA Admin: Dashboard > Connector Status. Compare with Splunk.
(b) Access an application and verify the latency metric in Splunk.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard: Row 1 — Connector health by segment. Row 2 — Access latency by app. Row 3 — SLA compliance.
Alert: Immediate when any segment drops below minimum connectors.

Runbook:
1. **Degraded segment** — Check connector host. Restart ZPA connector service.
2. **High latency** — Connector may be overloaded. Add connectors or increase host resources.
3. **All connectors offline** — Network outage at the connector site. Failover to secondary site.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting
• **No connector data** — LSS connector log type may not be enabled. Check ZPA LSS configuration.
• **Latency data missing** — User activity logs may not include latency fields. Check LSS log format.
grandmaExplanation: Our private applications are accessed through special connectors instead of a VPN. We monitor whether enough connectors are running for each application and how fast they respond, because if a connector goes down, people cannot reach the application even though they have permission.
